does: This paper identifies and addresses hallucination-to-action conversion, where multimodal agents' false perceptual claims enable unauthorized tool executions. The authors propose Evidence-Carrying Agents (ECA), a system that treats model text as inadmissible evidence and instead requires typed certificates from constrained verifiers (DOM, OCR, accessibility trees) for each action-critical predicate before tool execution. ECA implements a deterministic authorization gate that only permits actions supported by obtained certificates, converting opaque model beliefs into auditable residuals across verifier, schema, and implementation layers. Through red-teaming across 17 attack categories, they demonstrate that four specific hardening steps are each necessary, achieving zero canonical gate bypasses in 1,700 attempts and zero unsafe executions across 200 test cases.
connects: This work builds on growing recognition that multimodal language models face a distinct failure mode when deployed as autonomous agents interacting with external systems. It extends prior research on hallucination in language models by showing that in agentic contexts, hallucination becomes a security vulnerability rather than merely a quality issue. The paper connects to earlier work on tool use, grounding, and verification in agent systems, while drawing inspiration from formal verification and security hardening approaches in systems design. It responds to the challenge of making agent decision-making auditable and trustworthy when models generate free-form text that must drive real actions on user systems.
enables: This framework opens pathways toward trustworthy autonomous agents that can safely interact with complex digital environments. By making verification requirements explicit and evidence concrete, ECA enables further research into composable safety guarantees for multimodal agents and multi-step tasks. The approach suggests broader principles for sandboxing model outputs in high-stakes domains such as finance, healthcare, and infrastructure control. Future work could extend certificate-based authorization to multi-agent scenarios, integrate with formal verification methods, and develop more sophisticated verifiers for domain-specific predicates, potentially creating auditable decision trees for agent authorization across diverse application domains.
